title: README benchmark corpus link no longer downloads
author: m-novotny
body:
The benchmark section links to the OpenSubtitles v2018 English corpus, but the current URL no longer serves the file:

https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/blob/d5b85d44057ff729a89be9c6549958c45d95aa99/README.md#L75

### Reproduction

```console
$ curl -I -L --max-time 30 'http://opus.nlpl.eu/download.php?f=OpenSubtitles/v2018/mono/OpenSubtitles.raw.en.gz'
# redirects to HTTPS, then times out without returning the corpus
```

The OPUS object-storage URL for the same v2018 English monolingual corpus is live and returns `200 OK`:

```console
$ curl -I 'https://object.pouta.csc.fi/OPUS-OpenSubtitles/v2018/mono/en.txt.gz'
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
content-length: 3663376519
```

Would it make sense to replace the old `download.php` URL with:

`https://object.pouta.csc.fi/OPUS-OpenSubtitles/v2018/mono/en.txt.gz`

This is a recurrence of the dead-corpus-link problem from #1474, but I did not find an open issue for the current URL.

title: Homebrew-installed OpenAI Codex macOS app rg: Apple could not verify "rg" is free of malware that may harm you Mac or compromise your privacy
author: erikhuizinga
body: ### Please tick this box to confirm you have reviewed the above. - [x] I have a different issue. ### What version of ripgrep are you using? Copy | Version | Trust state -- | -- | -- /opt/homebrew/bin/rg | 15.1.0 | Homebrew copy; runs normally /Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/rg | 15.1.0 rev af60c2de9d | Signed by OpenAI OpCo, LLC; runs normally /opt/homebrew/Caskroom/codex/0.144.1/codex-path/rg | Not runnable due to dialog (see below) | Quarantined and only ad-hoc signed ### How did you install ripgrep? Homebrew, though I think this is not the version triggering macOS to show me the alert dialog. I also use the ChatGPT app for macOS (until recently it was called Codex), version 26.707.31428 and it seems that it ships with a version of ripgrep. And I might have another stale (?) Codex version installed on my system: see table above. ### What operating system are you using ripgrep on? macOS 26.5.1 (25F80) ### Describe your bug. A macOS potential malware alert dialog pops up and blocks rg use: ### What are the steps to reproduce the behavior? Codex runs tasks and internally uses an rg version that it ships with. I installed Codex and ChatGPT through various unofficial means, i.e. Homebrew. I'll try to uninstall them and reinstall them through official means now. I hope this will solve this problem by removing the stale (?) version of rg (and Codex/ChatGPT) from my system. This issue may be closed right away if this is indeed not a ripgrep issue. I'm only opening this for visibility, as macOS only mentions rg; other users might look here to file an issue or to search for a solution. ### What is the actual behavior? macOS blocks rg use and its commands therefore time out. ### What is the expected behavior? rg is safe to use and just runs, regardless of up-to-date installations by the human user or other vendors.

title: Read input paths from a file or stdin
author: ltrzesniewski
body: I'd like to suggest a new option, such as `--input=INPUTFILE` or `--input-file=INPUTFILE` (name TBD, the shorter the better), very similar to the existing `--file=PATTERNFILE`, which would read the list of files or directories to be searched from `INPUTFILE`. The value `-` would mean the list should be read from stdin. Here are some use cases: - Search from an existing (potentially long) list of files, which was previously generated, and could be reused across several `rg` calls. - Search for files containing multiple patterns, but not necessarily on the same line, by chaining `rg --files-with-matches` calls. As an example: searching for files containing `foo`, `bar` and `baz`: ```bash rg foo -l | rg bar --input=- -l | rg baz --input=- ``` The output would be a nicely presented list of `baz` matches, with headings. This also shows why a short option name would be useful. This would be an equivalent to the following on Linux: ```bash rg -0l foo | xargs -0 rg bar -0l | xargs -0 rg baz ``` But my main usage is on Windows, which doesn't have `xargs`, so the same command on PowerShell would be: ```powershell rg baz (rg -l bar (rg -l foo)) ``` Not only is it less readable (IMO), but it also doesn't support *long lists of files* to search from, as command-line arguments are limited to 32 kb on Windows: - Probably some other usages from #875 Of course, `--input=-` would have to be incompatible with `--file=-` and stdin input. I know you don't like adding more options, and for full disclosure I already have a script which handles this, but I believe this would be a useful feature for many people. If you'd like, I think I can write and submit a *simple* implementation in `rg::flags::hiargs::Paths` which would be based on `rg::flags::hiargs::Patterns` (which handles `--file`). The main issue I see with this approach is that stdin would need to be drained before the search begins, but this is already the case with `xargs` anyway. I just wanted to check with you first if that's something you'd be willing to accept.

title: cache `rg --pre` searches
author: SuperCowProducts
body: Thanks to some help at https://github.com/phiresky/ripgrep-all/issues/ I came up with a way to [search PDF annotations](https://github.com/phiresky/ripgrep-all/issues/294#issuecomment-4833092478), but I am missing cache, which is critical to enabling fast search in my library - what options have I got?

title: [ignore] Include depth in WithPath error.
author: tmccombs
body:
#### Describe your feature request

https://github.com/sharkdp/fd/issues/1017 would be significantly easier to fix if `ignore::Error::WithPath` included the depth, relative to the root of the path the error occurred on.  Specifically, in the event we get an error for a broken symlink, it allows us to know the depth of the broken symlink, which informs whether or not we should output anything for it.

title: GUIDE.md searches for lexopt in ripgrep 0.7.1 but 0.7.1 uses clap
author: MarlonBando
body:
In [GUIDE.md](https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/blob/master/GUIDE.md) the first step is to download version 0.7.1 and run the examples on that folder. 
Some examples search for `lexopt`, but in version 0.7.1 ripgrep was using `clap`. Therefore, when following the guide, the examples weren't working.
So either [GUIDE.md](https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/blob/master/GUIDE.md) should be updated to search for `clap` again, or the version being downloaded needs to be more recent, though this may break other examples.

[GUIDE.md](https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/blob/master/GUIDE.md) was updated to search for `lexopt` instead of `clap` in commit [c28ce76.](https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/commit/c28ce760dfbb16e284c1aa09d848cc016ae3fe88)

title: Double encoding of Hyperlink URL when file path contains whitespace and Cyrillic symbols
author: vgorloff
body:
### Please tick this box to confirm you have reviewed the above.

- [x] I have a different issue.

### What version of ripgrep are you using?

```sh
$ rg --version
ripgrep 15.1.0

features:+pcre2
simd(compile):+NEON
simd(runtime):+NEON

PCRE2 10.45 is available (JIT is available)
```

### How did you install ripgrep?

```
$ brew install rg
```

### What operating system are you using ripgrep on?

```sh
$ sw_vers
ProductName: macOS
ProductVersion: 26.5.1
BuildVersion: 25F80
```

### Describe your bug.

When path contains whitespace and Cyrillic symbols, the Hyperlink in search results points to non existing file. This seems due URL double encoding.

Issue observed in iTerm2. VSCode Terminal works fine. But, seems like VSCode Terminal works unfair, by doing double decoding Hyperlink URL.

### What are the steps to reproduce the behavior?

#### Create test files

```sh
mkdir "Test Folder"
echo -e "Hello\nWorld" > "Test Folder/abc.txt"
echo -e "Hello\nWorld" > "Test Folder/абв.txt"

mkdir "Other_Folder"
echo -e "Hello\nWorld" > "Other_Folder/abc.txt"
echo -e "Hello\nWorld" > "Other_Folder/абв.txt"
```

#### Perform search

```sh
rg --hyperlink-format=file "World"
```

#### Get results by copying iTerm2 links (right click -> Copy link address)

```sh
Test Folder/абв.txt # file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Test%2520Folder/%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B2.txt
2:World

Test Folder/abc.txt # file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Test%20Folder/abc.txt
2:World

Other_Folder/абв.txt # file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Other_Folder/%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B2.txt
2:World

Other_Folder/abc.txt # file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Other_Folder/abc.txt
2:World
```

### What is the actual behavior?

```sh
rg --hyperlink-format=file --debug "World"

rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::config|crates/core/flags/config.rs:41: /Users/vova/Preferences/Tools/rg/ripgreprc: arguments loaded from config file: ["--type-add=mcaAppleSDK:*.{tbd,apinotes,swiftinterface}"]
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:954: read CWD from environment: /Users/vova/Downloads/xxx
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1092: number of paths given to search: 0
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1117: using heuristics to determine whether to read from stdin or search ./ (is_readable_stdin=false, stdin_consumed=false, mode=Search(Standard))
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1127: heuristic chose to search ./
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1278: found hostname for hyperlink configuration: MCA
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1288: hyperlink format: "file://{host}{path}"
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:175: using 12 thread(s)
rg: DEBUG|grep_regex::config|/private/tmp/ripgrep-20251023-8789-373h74/ripgrep-15.1.0/crates/regex/src/config.rs:175: assembling HIR from 1 fixed string literals
rg: DEBUG|ignore::gitignore|crates/ignore/src/gitignore.rs:398: opened gitignore file: /Users/vova/.gitignore_global
rg: DEBUG|globset|crates/globset/src/lib.rs:515: built glob set; 1 literals, 1 basenames, 0 extensions, 0 prefixes, 1 suffixes, 0 required extensions, 0 regexes
Other_Folder/абв.txt
2:World

Test Folder/abc.txt
2:World

Test Folder/абв.txt
2:World

Other_Folder/abc.txt
2:World
```

### What is the expected behavior?

The Hyperlink URL for search result `Test Folder/абв.txt`:

- Observed: `file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Test%2520Folder/%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B2.txt`
- Expected: `file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Test%20Folder/%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B2.txt`

Seems like a double URL encoding takes place. `Test Folder` encoded first to `Test%20Folder`, and then encoded one more time to `Test%2520Folder`.

#### URL encoding example (via https://www.urlencoder.org)

```
Test Folder   => Test%20Folder
Test%20Folder => Test%2520Folder
```

iTerm2 seems behaves correctly (right click -> Copy link address). Below commands producing correct URLs:

```sh
$ printf '\e]8;;file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Test Folder/абв.txt\e\\This is a file link\e]8;;\e\\\n'
file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Test%20Folder/%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B2.txt

$ printf '\e]8;;file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Test%%20Folder/%%D0%%B0%%D0%%B1%%D0%%B2.txt\e\\This is a file link\e]8;;\e\\\n'
file://MCA/Users/vova/Downloads/xxx/Test%20Folder/%D0%B0%D0%B1%D0%B2.txt
```

author: Groz17

Emit submatches

title: Search inside a ignored directory still uses ignore file
author: isaacl
body:
### Please tick this box to confirm you have reviewed the above.

- [x] I have a different issue.

### What version of ripgrep are you using?

ripgrep 15.1.0

features:+pcre2
simd(compile):+NEON
simd(runtime):+NEON

PCRE2 10.45 is available (JIT is available)

### How did you install ripgrep?

Homebrew

### What operating system are you using ripgrep on?

macOS 26.5

### Describe your bug.

I was looking for the source of a log statement from one of my node_modules.   I cd'ed  to `node_modules/<package>` and did an rg search.  I got some results, but not my match.  Why?   Because the file got ignored due to my repo .gitignore, which looked like
```
node_modules/
dist/
```

I was inside node_modules, an ignored directory.  My gitignore hadn't been written to filter files here, the whole folder was already ignored.   But rg found the `.gitignore` and applied it.

### What are the steps to reproduce the behavior?

```
mkdir repo; cd repo
printf '%s\n' dist node_modules > .gitignore
mkdir .git node_modules; cd node_modules
echo foo > dist
rg foo
```

### What is the actual behavior?

no match

### What is the expected behavior?

rg should not use an ignore file for file trees that the ignore file would drop.

title: rg reads outer repo gitignore even though it ignores it
author: isaacl
body:
### Please tick this box to confirm you have reviewed the above.

- [x] I have a different issue.

### What version of ripgrep are you using?

ripgrep 15.1.0

features:+pcre2
simd(compile):+NEON
simd(runtime):+NEON

PCRE2 10.45 is available (JIT is available)

### How did you install ripgrep?

Homebrew.  Also tested on master.

### What operating system are you using ripgrep on?

mac latest

### Describe your bug.

rg is processing a file it doesn't use (gitignore).  This leads to confusing debugging because it creates globs it does not apply to the file walk.

### What are the steps to reproduce the behavior?

```
mkdir outer; cd outer
echo bar > .gitignore
mkdir inner; cd inner
mkdir .git; echo foo > bar
```


### What is the actual behavior?

rg correctly ignores the outer gitignore, since it's past the git boundary
```
/tmp/outer/inner $ rg foo
bar
1:foo
```

But it still processes it, and lists it.  Why?
```
/tmp/outer/inner $ rg --debug foo 2>&1 | grep -F .gitignore
rg: DEBUG|ignore::gitignore|crates/ignore/src/gitignore.rs:398: opened gitignore file: /private/tmp/outer/.gitignore
rg: DEBUG|globset|crates/globset/src/lib.rs:527: glob set patterns — literals: [], basenames: ["bar"], extensions: [], prefixes: [], suffixes: [], required_extensions: [], regexes: []
```
(the last line is a custom log I added)

### What is the expected behavior?

ripgrep should not open outer repo gitignore when it's past git boundary.  For performance and also for easier debugging.

title: Avoid redundant string allocation in path filtering logic
author: ShinysLove
body:
While profiling large codebase searches, I noticed redundant heap allocations occurring inside the directory traversal loop when evaluating ignore/include path filters. Specifically, paths are occasionally converted into owned strings (`Path::to_string_lossy().into_owned()`) before matching, even when a borrowed check against `&Path` or &`str` would suffice.

**Solution:**
- [ ] Refactor the filtering pipeline to use borrowed path slices where possible, or leverage `Cow<'a, str>` to defer allocation unless a transformation is strictly necessary.


> Reducing allocations in the hot path of directory walking will slightly improve performance during massive parallel searches.

title: rg stdout documentation: mechanism to enable pagination.
author: gigaimage
body:
#### Describe your feature request

Unless I completely missed the feature requested here is there already , rg documentation stdout exits out at the bottom of last page (e.g. rg --help OR rg --type-list etc.). It'd be nice to able to enable some sort of pagination,  something like built-in pagination in man pages.

Of course, a workaround is to pipe stdout to bat/less, but still.

title: Inconsistent behaviour between configuration file and passing arguments on the commandline
author: fenree
body:
Unsure whether to tag this is a bug or a feature request or a bit of both.

## Exposition
Running Arch Linux fully up to date, everything is tested using bash --norc
```bash
zephyr@archlinux
~ $ bash --norc
bash-5.3$ bash --version
GNU bash, version 5.3.9(1)-release (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
Copyright (C) 2025 Free Software Foundation, Inc.
License GPLv3+: GNU GPL version 3 or later <http://gnu.org/licenses/gpl.html>

This is free software; you are free to change and redistribute it.
There is NO WARRANTY, to the extent permitted by law.
bash-5.3$ rg --version
ripgrep 15.1.0

features:+pcre2
simd(compile):+SSE2,-SSSE3,-AVX2
simd(runtime):+SSE2,+SSSE3,+AVX2

PCRE2 10.45 is available (JIT is available)
```


### The issue (part 1)
```bash
bash-5.3$ cat $RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH
-g '!.org/*'
bash-5.3$ rg 67
rg: No files were searched, which means ripgrep probably applied a filter you didn't expect.
Running with --debug will show why files are being skipped.
bash-5.3$ mv $RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH{,.backup}
bash-5.3$ rg -g '!.org/*' 67 | head -n 1
rg: failed to read the file specified in RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH: /home/zephyr/.rgrc: No such file or directory (os error 2)
org/git.org:    ':(exclude)*mx[678]*'          \
```
If i use single quotes in my config file it will not work. This is a problem because in bash, which is the shell that is most commonly used as it is the default, you cannot use the -g option to exclude a directory without using singlequotes. This is because the exclamation mark is a special character in bash with default settings:

```bash
bash-5.3$ rg -g !.org/*
bash: !.org/: event not found
```
This also occurs in zsh actually
```bash
zephyr @ archlinux
~ $ zsh
archlinux% rg -g !.org/* pat
zsh: event not found: .org/
```

Bash users, are then going to use singlequotes every single time, because without them it doesn't work. 
Ofcourse this happens because when ripgrep reads arguments on the commandline it expects the shell to have already stripped the quotes, as a POSIX shell will do that. However when it reads from a file, those arguments are not intercepted by a shell at all, so not only are they unnecessary, but they also do not get removed. This however, means that ripgrep should be able to handle that case. Instead what happens is that it will tell you to use the debug flag, which only really tells you that the glob is ignoring every file, but not _why_ the glob is ignoring them.

## The issue (part 2)

This is inconsistent and confusing behaviour. What makes it a really big issue is that the manpage doesn't mention this behaviour, it just lists an example without quotes.

```
     ripgrep  will  look for a single configuration file if and only if
     the RIPGREP_CONFIG_PATH environment variable is set  and  is  non-
     empty.  ripgrep will parse arguments from this file on startup and
     will behave as if the arguments in this file were prepended to any
     explicit  arguments  given  to  ripgrep  on the command line. 

     ...

     The same applies to using globs. This:

         --glob=!.git

     or this:

         --glob
         !.git

     would behave identically to the following command:

         rg --glob '!.git' foo

     The bottom line is that every shell argument needs to  be  on  its
     own line. So for example, a config file containing
```

No mention of this behaviour at all in the manpage. People who don't know exactly which order shells parse things and hand them off to commands in, could spend quite a while scratching their heads with this one. One could argue whether the solution is making the behaviour consistent, i. e. ripgrep being able to strip quotes from the configuration file arguments like the shell would, or maybe one could just add a warning for this edge case, or even just tweak the manpage so atleast the manpage doesn't outright state that the configuration files arguments behave as though they are prepended to the explicit arguments on the commandline, as that is false and misleading.

Happy to provide any other details if necessary.
Thank you for reading and thank you for your time. Ripgrep is awesome.

author: jelle-openai

title: ignore: gitignore in subpath is used from parent
author: inqode-lars
body:
I see a difference in the behaviour in git and in the ignore crrate.

The following test is pointing it out:
https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep/blob/4519153e5e461527f4bca45b042fff45c4ec6fb9/crates/ignore/src/walk.rs#L2255

As far as i understand git would not ignore a/foo because it is not ignored in that directory level.
But the ignore crate is ignoring a/foo.
I undestand that it would make sense for patterns, but i don't think that this is the expected behaviour for file names.

title: Add hierarchy-aware mode to include hierarchical parent context in search results
author: layzerar
body:
I’d like to propose a new `--hierarchy` option for ripgrep that prints hierarchical parent lines for each match, giving search results more useful context without substantially increasing output size.

For example, given `test.md`:

```md
list content:
  1. list item (level 1)
  2. list item (level 1)
    - inner list item: foobar (level 2)
```

Running:

```sh
rg --hierarchy foobar test.md
```

could produce:

```text
test.md
1: list content:
3:   2. list item (level 1)
4:     - inner list item: foobar (level 2)
```

The idea is that when a matching line is found, ripgrep would also print the relevant ancestor lines that establish its hierarchy. In this example, the match is inside a nested Markdown list, so the output includes the top-level context line and the parent list item.

This would be useful for more than Markdown. Many common file formats and programming languages encode useful structure through indentation or simple block hierarchy, including Python, C, C++, Java, YAML, TOML, configuration files, logs with grouped sections, and documentation formats. Current context options like `-A`, `-B`, and `-C` are line-distance based, which can be noisy or miss the actual structural context. A hierarchy-aware mode could provide more precise context with fewer extra lines.

The implementation would not need to perform full syntax parsing. A simple indentation/block-based heuristic would already be valuable:

- Track previous lines that appear to be structural ancestors.
- When a match is found, emit the nearest relevant parent lines before the matching line.
- Deduplicate ancestor lines when multiple matches share the same hierarchy.
- Preserve normal ripgrep behavior unless `--hierarchy` is explicitly enabled.

This should keep the feature relatively non-invasive and avoid significant performance impact compared with language-specific parsing. It would provide a middle ground between minimal search output and verbose surrounding-context output, making ripgrep results easier to understand when matches occur inside nested structures.

title: no output from (truncated zstd) compressed files
author: phord
body:
### Please tick this box to confirm you have reviewed the above.

- [x] I have a different issue.

### What version of ripgrep are you using?

ripgrep 15.1.0

### How did you install ripgrep?

cargo install ripgrep

### What operating system are you using ripgrep on?

Ubuntu 20.04

### Describe your bug.

ripgrep returns no matches in compressed files when the decompressor exits with a nonzero exit code and multiple files are searched in parallel.  It also fails to count matches in a single file when an error code is returned from the decompressor.

I have several .zst files, all of which were closed uncleanly (end-frame is corrupt).  When I decompress them, they show millions of lines, but the decompressor reports an error.  This is expected.

    $ zstdcat foo.zst | wc -l
    foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end 
    3666594
    $ zstdcat bar.zst | wc -l
    bar.zst : Read error (39) : premature end 
    3666594

    $ zstdcat foo.zst > /dev/null || echo $?
    foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end 
    1

The following things incorrectly fail to produce any results:
- Counting results:   `rg -zc needle foo.zst` 
- Searching multiple files in parallel:  `rg -z needle foo.zst bar.zst`

By contrast, these succeed:
- Using zstdcat to decompress and piping this to rg:   `zstdcat foo.zst | rg -c needle` 
- Searching multiple files with --sort:  `rg -z needle foo.zst bar.zst --sort=path` 
- Searching a single file:  `rg -z needle foo.zst` 

```
    $ rg -zc "memory locking enabled" foo.zst
    rg: foo.zst: 
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------


    $ rg -z "memory locking enabled" foo.zst bar.zst

    rg: foo.zst: 
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    rg: bar.zst: 
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    bar.zst : Read error (39) : premature end
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------


    $ zstdcat foo.zst | rg -c "memory locking enabled"

    foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end 
    1


    $ rg -z "memory locking enabled" foo.zst bar.zst --sort=path
    foo.zst
    2:Apr 25 17:13:26.023 000000000135 I      osenv.posix memory locking enabled
    rg: foo.zst: 
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    bar.zst
    2:Apr 25 17:13:26.023 000000000135 I      osenv.posix memory locking enabled
    rg: bar.zst: 
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    bar.zst : Read error (39) : premature end
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    $ rg -z "memory locking enabled" foo.zst                    
    2:Apr 25 17:13:26.023 000000000135 I      osenv.posix memory locking enabled
    rg: foo.zst: 
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------

```

### What are the steps to reproduce the behavior?

```
# Create a compressed file with end-of-file damage
$ cat /usr/share/dict/words | zstd -o foo.zst
/*stdin*\            : 30.64%   (   962 KiB =>    295 KiB, foo.zst)            

$ ls -l foo.zst       
-rw-rw-r-- 1 phord phord 301788 Apr 25 12:39 foo.zst

$ truncate --size=301700 foo.zst

$ zstdcat foo.zst | wc 
foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end 
  97093   97094  917504

$ zstdcat foo.zst > /dev/null || echo $?
foo.zst : Read error (39) : premature end 
1


# Searching in one file works
$ rg -z aardvarks foo.zst --no-messages 
20498:aardvarks

# Searching in two files fails
$ rg -z aardvarks foo.zst foo.zst --no-messages

# Unless they're sorted (single-threaded)
$ rg -z aardvarks foo.zst foo.zst --no-messages --sort=path
foo.zst
20498:aardvarks

foo.zst
20498:aardvarks

# Counting always fails
$ rg -cz aardvarks foo.zst --no-messages

```

### What is the actual behavior?

ripgrep gives up on files when an error is returned from the decompressor.

### What is the expected behavior?

I expect rg to successfully search the ouput of decompressors even when an error is eventually encountered.


----

This is arguably a bug in zstd since gzip doesn't exit with an error when the same truncation error is encountered.

title: `.gitignore` not taken into account when multiple search paths are provided
author: Erwyn
body:
### Please tick this box to confirm you have reviewed the above.

- [x] I have a different issue.

### What version of ripgrep are you using?

ripgrep 15.1.0

features:+pcre2
simd(compile):+SSE2,-SSSE3,-AVX2
simd(runtime):+SSE2,+SSSE3,+AVX2

PCRE2 10.45 is available (JIT is available)

### How did you install ripgrep?

Nixpkgs stable 25.11

### What operating system are you using ripgrep on?

NixOs 25.11

### Describe your bug.

`.gitignore` seems to be not taken into account if mutiple search paths are provided

### What are the steps to reproduce the behavior?

1. `mkdir tests src`
2. `echo "this" > tests/valid && echo "this" > src/valid && echo "this" > src/invalid`
3. `git init . && echo "src/invalid" > .gitignore`
4. `rg "this"` => no `src/invalid`
5. `rg "this" src/` => no `src/invalid`
6. `rg "this" src/ tests/` => **`src/invalid` is found**
7. `rg "this" tests/ src/` => funnily, `src/invalid` is not found

### What is the actual behavior?

```
❯ rg --debug "this" src tests
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::parse|crates/core/flags/parse.rs:97: no extra arguments found from configuration file
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:954: read CWD from environment: /tmp/reproduce
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1092: number of paths given to search: 2
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1103: is_one_file? false
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1278: found hostname for hyperlink configuration: sunbase
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:1288: hyperlink format: ""
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|crates/core/flags/hiargs.rs:175: using 12 thread(s)
rg: DEBUG|grep_regex::config|/build/source/crates/regex/src/config.rs:175: assembling HIR from 1 fixed string literals
rg: DEBUG|ignore::gitignore|crates/ignore/src/gitignore.rs:398: opened gitignore file: /tmp/reproduce/.gitignore
rg: DEBUG|globset|crates/globset/src/lib.rs:515: built glob set; 1 literals, 0 basenames, 0 extensions, 0 prefixes, 0 suffixes, 0 required extensions, 0 regexes
rg: DEBUG|ignore::gitignore|crates/ignore/src/gitignore.rs:398: opened gitignore file: /tmp/reproduce/.git/info/exclude
src/valid
1:this

src/invalid
1:this

tests/valid
1:this
```

### What is the expected behavior?

In step `6.` of reproduction I feel that I should not find `src/invalid`

title: Panic in windows cmd.exe with piped input
author: teamunicycle
body:
### Please tick this box to confirm you have reviewed the above.

- [x] I have a different issue.

### What version of ripgrep are you using?

ripgrep 15.1.0

### How did you install ripgrep?

cargo install

### What operating system are you using ripgrep on?

windows 11, 25H2

### Describe your bug.

ripgrep panics 


### What are the steps to reproduce the behavior?

Windows 11, cmd.exe. Doesn't happen in powershell.

piped help from eza command into rg with no parameters:

`eza --help | rg`

Works fine if there's actually an expression after rg, which is of course what I meant to do
`eza --help | rg git`
Works fine with other programs writing to stdout.

### What is the actual behavior?

>eza --help | rg --debug
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::parse|C:\Users\Me\.cargo\registry\src\index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f\ripgrep-15.1.0\crates\core\flags\parse.rs:97: no extra arguments found from configuration file
rg: DEBUG|rg::flags::hiargs|C:\Users\Me\.cargo\registry\src\index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f\ripgrep-15.1.0\crates\core\flags\hiargs.rs:954: read CWD from environment: C:\Users\Me
rg: ripgrep requires at least one pattern to execute a search

thread 'main' (80452) panicked at /rustc/e408947bfd200af42db322daf0fadfe7e26d3bd1/library\std\src\io\stdio.rs:1165:9:
failed printing to stdout: The pipe is being closed. (os error 232)
stack backtrace:
   0:     0x7ff6c43db900 - <unknown>
   1:     0x7ff6c43b21b1 - <unknown>
   2:     0x7ff6c43dac74 - <unknown>
   3:     0x7ff6c43da895 - <unknown>
   4:     0x7ff6c43e1775 - <unknown>
   5:     0x7ff6c43e171f - <unknown>
   6:     0x7ff6c43e170e - <unknown>
   7:     0x7ff6c44cf8ed - git_trace_set
   8:     0x7ff6c435bf94 - <unknown>
   9:     0x7ff6c4382796 - <unknown>
  10:     0x7ff6c43ae325 - <unknown>
  11:     0x7ff6c44cc29c - git_trace_set
  12:     0x7ff8afb1e8d7 - BaseThreadInitThunk
  13:     0x7ff8b098c48c - RtlUserThreadStart


### What is the expected behavior?

Should have just told me to include a pattern in the command
